Front: When did Henry VII become king?
Back: 1485 after defeating Richard III at the Battle of Bosworth

Front: Why was Henry VII's claim to the throne weak?
Back: It came through the Beaufort line which had been barred from succession

Front: What was the significance of the Battle of Bosworth?
Back: It ended the Wars of the Roses and established Tudor rule

Front: Why did Henry VII marry Elizabeth of York?
Back: To unite the houses of Lancaster and York and strengthen his claim

Front: What was the purpose of the Tudor Rose?
Back: It symbolised the union of York and Lancaster

Front: Who was Lambert Simnel?
Back: A pretender claiming to be the Earl of Warwick

Front: What happened to Lambert Simnel?
Back: He was defeated at the Battle of Stoke in 1487

Front: Why was the Battle of Stoke significant?
Back: It was the final battle of the Wars of the Roses

Front: Who was Perkin Warbeck?
Back: A pretender claiming to be Richard Duke of York

Front: Why was Perkin Warbeck a serious threat?
Back: He gained foreign support from Burgundy and Scotland

Front: What role did Margaret of Burgundy play?
Back: She supported Yorkist pretenders against Henry VII

Front: What were bonds and recognisances?
Back: Financial penalties used to control the nobility

Front: Why did Henry VII use bonds and recognisances?
Back: To ensure loyalty and obedience from the nobility

Front: What was the Council Learned in the Law?
Back: A body that enforced royal financial rights

Front: Why was the Council Learned unpopular?
Back: It aggressively collected money through bonds and fines

Front: What was Henry VII's policy towards the nobility?
Back: To reduce their power and prevent overmighty subjects

Front: What was the significance of the Treaty of Medina del Campo?
Back: It secured an alliance with Spain in 1489

Front: Who married Catherine of Aragon?
Back: Arthur Tudor first then Henry VIII

Front: Why was Arthur's marriage politically important?
Back: It strengthened England's alliance with Spain

Front: What financial policies strengthened Henry VII's rule?
Back: Efficient taxation feudal dues and customs duties

Front: Why was Henry VII cautious in foreign policy?
Back: He prioritised financial stability and security

Front: What was the role of Justices of the Peace?
Back: Local officials maintaining law and order

Front: How did Henry VII strengthen royal authority locally?
Back: By expanding the role of Justices of the Peace

Front: What was the purpose of the Star Chamber?
Back: To deal with powerful nobles outside normal courts

Front: When did Henry VIII become king?
Why was Henry VIII initially popular?
Back: He represented a break from Henry VII's cautious rule

Front: Who was Cardinal Wolsey?
Back: Henry VIII's chief minister from 1515 to 1529

Front: What was the significance of the Field of the Cloth of Gold?
Back: A diplomatic meeting between Henry VIII and Francis I

Front: Why did Wolsey fall from power?
Back: He failed to secure Henry's divorce from Catherine of Aragon

Front: What was the Great Matter?
Back: Henry VIII's attempt to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon

Front: Why did Henry VIII want a divorce?
Back: He lacked a male heir and wanted to marry Anne Boleyn

Front: What was the role of the Pope in the divorce crisis?
Back: Only the Pope could grant an annulment

Front: Why did the Pope refuse Henry's request?
Back: He was under pressure from Emperor Charles V

Front: What was the Act of Supremacy 1534?
Back: It made Henry VIII Supreme Head of the Church of England

Front: Why was the Act of Supremacy significant?
Back: It broke England from papal authority

Front: What was the Dissolution of the Monasteries?
Back: The closure and confiscation of monastic institutions

Front: Why did Henry dissolve the monasteries?
Back: To increase royal wealth and power

Front: What was the Pilgrimage of Grace?
Back: A northern rebellion against religious reforms in 1536

Front: Why did the Pilgrimage of Grace occur?
Back: Opposition to dissolution and religious change

Front: How did Henry respond to the Pilgrimage of Grace?
Back: He suppressed it and executed leaders

Front: Who was Thomas Cromwell?
Back: Henry VIII's chief minister who drove religious reforms

Front: What administrative reforms did Cromwell introduce?
Back: Expansion of royal bureaucracy and financial courts

Front: What happened to Anne Boleyn?
Back: She was executed in 1536

Front: Why was Anne Boleyn executed?
Back: Accused of adultery treason and incest

Front: What religious changes occurred under Edward VI?
Back: Protestant reforms such as the Book of Common Prayer

Front: Who ruled during Edward VI's minority?
Back: A regency council dominated by Somerset then Northumberland

Front: What was the Prayer Book Rebellion?
Back: A rebellion against Protestant religious changes in 1549

Front: What was Kett's Rebellion?
Back: A rebellion in Norfolk over enclosure and economic grievances

Front: Why was Edward VI's reign unstable?
Back: Religious change and social unrest

Front: Why did Mary I become queen in 1553?
Back: She had strong hereditary legitimacy

Front: What religion did Mary I restore?
Back: Roman Catholicism

Front: Why is Mary known as Bloody Mary?
Back: She executed Protestants during her reign

Front: What was Mary's marriage alliance?
Back: She married Philip II of Spain

Front: Why was Mary's marriage unpopular?
Back: Fear of Spanish influence and loss of independence

Front: What was the significance of the Marian persecutions?
Back: They created Protestant martyrs and strengthened Protestant identity

Front: When did Elizabeth I become queen?
What was the Elizabethan Religious Settlement?
Back: A compromise establishing Protestantism

Front: What did the Act of Supremacy 1559 state?
Back: Elizabeth was Supreme Governor of the Church

Front: What did the Act of Uniformity require?
Back: Use of the Book of Common Prayer

Front: What were recusants?
Back: People who refused to attend Anglican services

Front: Why did Catholics threaten Elizabeth?
Back: They supported Mary Queen of Scots as a rival claimant

Front: Who was Mary Queen of Scots?
Back: Elizabeth's Catholic cousin and rival

Front: Why was Mary executed in 1587?
Back: She was involved in Catholic plots against Elizabeth

Front: What was the Spanish Armada?
Back: A Spanish invasion fleet sent against England in 1588

Front: Why did Spain attack England?
Back: Religious conflict and English support for Dutch rebels

Front: Why did the Armada fail?
Back: Bad weather English naval tactics and logistical problems

Front: What was Elizabeth's foreign policy aim?
Back: To maintain stability and avoid major wars

Front: What was the significance of the defeat of the Armada?
Back: It boosted Elizabeth's prestige and national confidence

Front: What was the role of the Privy Council?
Back: Advising the monarch and administering government

Front: Who were Elizabeth's key ministers?
Back: William Cecil and Francis Walsingham

Front: What was the role of Parliament under the Tudors?
Back: To pass legislation and approve taxation

Front: Why did Elizabeth manage Parliament carefully?
Back: To maintain royal authority and avoid conflict

Front: What were the Poor Laws?
Back: Legislation to deal with poverty and unemployment

Front: Why did poverty increase in the late Tudor period?
Back: Population growth inflation and economic change

Front: What was enclosure?
Back: The fencing off of common land for private use

Front: Why did enclosure cause unrest?
Back: It displaced rural workers and increased poverty

Front: What was the significance of the Tudor period?
Back: It established stronger monarchy and religious change in England
